Press enter or click to view image in full size

A Chinese lab built a model the world wanted so badly it ran out of chips to serve everyone, and on July 27 it hands that model over for free. Washington is weighing five ways to restrict American companies from using Chinese AI, but open weights are a copy, not a shipment, and copies do not stop at borders. The tools were built for a world of ports; the fight has moved to a world of downloads.Member-only story

Artificial Intelligence

China

Technology

Open Source

Politics

8 min read
Just now

--

Moonshot AI stopped accepting new customers this week because too many people wanted its newest model. Six days from now, the company gives that same model away for free.
By The Daily Reflection · July 21, 2026 · 10 min read
The notice went up quietly, the way a restaurant flips a sign to say the kitchen is closed.
Moonshot AI, the Beijing startup behind the Kimi assistant, announced it was suspending new subscriptions. Not because of a security incident. Not because of a regulatory order. Demand for its new model, Kimi K3, had pushed the company to the edge of its available computing power in roughly 48 hours, and it could not physically serve anyone else. Existing subscribers would keep their access. Everyone else would have to wait while Moonshot added capacity “as fast as we can.”
In the same week, Axios reported that officials inside the Trump administration have been quietly arguing over how to stop American companies from using Chinese AI models exactly like this one.
Put those two facts next to each other and you get the most honest picture available of where the AI race actually stands: a Chinese lab too swamped with global demand to take your money, and an American…
